title: Soupe à l'Oignon
subtitle: French onion soup with caramelised onions, beef stock, and a Gruyère crouton
sourceNotes: Soupe à l'oignon is documented in French cookery from at least the eighteenth century. It appears in Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903) and was popularised in English-speaking countries by Julia Child's Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961). The Parisian version using beef stock and gratinéed with Gruyère is the most widely known.

content.text: This recipe is almost entirely about the

of the onions. Forty minutes at low heat is not optional. It is where all the flavour comes from. Onions cooked at medium-high heat for fifteen minutes are not caramelised; they are merely softened, and the soup will taste thin and sharp. Allow the time.

Melt {{butter-unsalted}} of butter with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large, wide pan over a medium-low heat. Add {{onion}} of finely sliced onions with a pinch of salt. Stir to coat. Cook for 40 to 50 minutes, stirring every 5 to 8 minutes, until deep golden and reduced to roughly a quarter of their starting volume. If any bits are catching on the base, add a splash of water and scrape.

Pour in {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine, increase the heat to medium, and let it bubble for 2 minutes. Add {{beef-stock}} of hot beef stock, the thyme sprigs, and the bay leaf. Season. Simmer for 15 minutes. Remove the herbs.

Toast the bread until dry and golden. Heat the grill to maximum. Ladle the soup into oven-safe bowls, float a crouton in each, and cover with {{gruyere}} of grated Gruyère. Place under the grill for 3 to 5 minutes until the cheese is bubbling and beginning to brown at the edges, this is the
